text: The surgeon begins lateral dissection by detaching the natural adhesions of the sigmoid colon, progressing towards the splenic flexure. The Told's fascia is separated from the mesocolon, revealing the left gonadal vessel and ureter beneath. The mesosigmoid is exposed by grasping the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, initiating medial dissection from the sacral promontory towards the IMA root. A peritoneal window is created between the lateral and medial dissection planes, followed by apical node dissection starting at the anticipated IMA bifurcation, where the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) traverses.

The surgeon carefully performs IMA node dissection, avoiding injury to the superior hypogastric nerve plexus and preaortic nerve plexus. Following the completion of the node dissection, the IMV is ligated, and the supravector artery is ligated at the IMA bifurcation while preserving the left colic artery.

The surgeon begins pelvic dissection from the sacral promontory, where abundant lusoreolar tissues allow easy separation of the rectal proper fascia from the pre-hypogastric nerve fascia. The mobilized rectum is suspended with a tape for traction. A U-shaped incision is made at the peritoneal reflection, revealing the seminal vesicles and nonvertebral fascia. During posterior lateral dissection, the hypogastric nerves and inferior hypogastric nerve plexus are carefully preserved. After incising the rectal sacral fascia, the pelvic floor becomes visible, and anterolateral dissection continues with caution to protect the neurovascular bundles leading to genitourinary organs.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum and divides the mesorectum at the distal margin. The rectum is resected using linear staplers. A mini-laparotomy site is prepared for specimen extraction.
